children.heading: .—Policy
children.label: 1 .—Policy
children.text: It is the policy of the United States to improve the condition and performance of the National Highway Freight Network established under this section to ensure that the Network provides the foundation for the United States to compete in the global economy and achieve the goals described in subsection (b).

children.heading: .—Establishment
children.label: 2 .—Establishment
children.text: In support of the goals described in subsection (b), the Administrator of the Federal Highway Administration shall establish a national highway freight program in accordance with this section to improve the efficient movement of freight on the National Highway Freight Network.

children.label: A
children.text: strengthen the contribution of the National Highway Freight Network to the economic competitiveness of the United States;

children.label: B
children.text: reduce congestion and bottlenecks on the National Highway Freight Network;

children.label: C
children.text: reduce the cost of freight transportation;

children.label: D
children.text: improve the year-round reliability of freight transportation; and

children.label: E
children.text: increase productivity, particularly for domestic industries and businesses that create high-value jobs;
to improve the safety, security, efficiency, and resiliency of freight transportation in rural and urban areas;
to improve the state of good repair of the National Highway Freight Network;
to use innovation and advanced technology to improve the safety, efficiency, and reliability of the National Highway Freight Network;
to improve the efficiency and productivity of the National Highway Freight Network;

to improve the flexibility of States to support multi-State corridor planning and the creation of multi-State organizations to increase the ability of States to address highway freight connectivity; and
to reduce the environmental impacts of freight movement on the National Highway Freight Network.

children.heading: Establishment of National Highway Freight Network.—
children.label: c Establishment of National Highway Freight Network.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: The Administrator shall establish a National Highway Freight Network in accordance with this section to strategically direct Federal resources and policies toward improved performance of the Network.

children.label: A
children.text: the primary highway freight system, as designated under subsection (d);

children.label: B
children.text: critical rural freight corridors established under subsection (e);

children.label: C
children.text: critical urban freight corridors established under subsection (f); and

children.label: D
children.text: the portions of the Interstate System not designated as part of the primary highway freight system.

children.heading: .—Designation and Redesignation of the Primary Highway Freight System
children.label: d .—Designation and Redesignation of the Primary Highway Freight System

children.heading: .—Initial designation of primary highway freight system
children.label: 1 .—Initial designation of primary highway freight system
children.text: The initial designation of the primary highway freight system shall be the 41,518-mile network identified during the designation process for the primary freight network under , as in effect on the day before the date of enactment of the FAST Act.section 167(d) of this title

children.heading: Redesignation of primary highway freight system.—
children.label: 2 Redesignation of primary highway freight system.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: A .—In general
children.text: Beginning 5 years after the date of enactment of the FAST Act, and every 5 years thereafter, using the designation factors described in subparagraph (E), the Administrator shall redesignate the primary highway freight system.

children.heading: .—Redesignation mileage
children.label: B .—Redesignation mileage
children.text: Each redesignation may increase the mileage on the primary highway freight system by not more than 3 percent of the total mileage of the system.

children.heading: .—Use of measurable data
children.label: C .—Use of measurable data
children.text: In redesignating the primary highway freight system, to the maximum extent practicable, the Administrator shall use measurable data to assess the significance of goods movement, including consideration of points of origin, destinations, and linking components of the United States global and domestic supply chains.

children.heading: .—Input
children.label: D .—Input
children.text: In redesignating the primary highway freight system, the Administrator shall provide an opportunity for State freight advisory committees, as applicable, to submit additional miles for consideration.

children.label: i
children.text: changes in the origins and destinations of freight movement in, to, and from the United States;

children.label: ii
children.text: changes in the percentage of annual daily truck traffic in the annual average daily traffic on principal arterials;

children.label: iii
children.text: changes in the location of key facilities;

children.label: iv
children.text: land and water ports of entry;

children.label: v
children.text: access to energy exploration, development, installation, or production areas;

children.label: vi
children.text: access to other freight intermodal facilities, including rail, air, water, and pipelines facilities;

children.label: vii
children.text: the total freight tonnage and value moved via highways;

children.label: viii
children.text: significant freight bottlenecks, as identified by the Administrator;

children.label: ix
children.text: the significance of goods movement on principal arterials, including consideration of global and domestic supply chains;

children.label: x
children.text: critical emerging freight corridors and critical commerce corridors; and

children.label: xi
children.text: network connectivity.

children.label: A
children.text: is a rural principal arterial roadway and has a minimum of 25 percent of the annual average daily traffic of the road measured in passenger vehicle equivalent units from trucks (Federal Highway Administration vehicle class 8 to 13);

children.label: B
children.text: provides access to energy exploration, development, installation, or production areas;

children.label: i
children.text: 50,000 20-foot equivalent units per year; or

children.label: ii
children.text: 500,000 tons per year of bulk commodities;

children.label: i
children.text: a grain elevator;

children.label: ii
children.text: an agricultural facility;

children.label: iii
children.text: a mining facility;

children.label: iv
children.text: a forestry facility; or

children.label: v
children.text: an intermodal facility;

children.label: E
children.text: connects to an international port of entry;

children.label: F
children.text: provides access to significant air, rail, water, or other freight facilities in the State; or

children.label: G
children.text: is, in the determination of the State, vital to improving the efficient movement of freight of importance to the economy of the State.

children.heading: .—Limitation
children.label: 2 .—Limitation
children.text: A State may designate as critical rural freight corridors a maximum of 300 miles of highway or 20 percent of the primary highway freight system mileage in the State, whichever is greater.

children.heading: .—Rural states
children.label: 3 .—Rural states
children.text: Notwithstanding paragraph (2), a State with a population per square mile of area that is less than the national average, based on the 2010 census, may designate as critical rural freight corridors a maximum of 600 miles of highway or 25 percent of the primary highway freight system mileage in the State, whichever is greater.

children.heading: .—Urbanized area with population of 500,000 or more
children.label: 1 .—Urbanized area with population of 500,000 or more
children.text: In an urbanized area with a population of 500,000 or more individuals, the representative metropolitan planning organization, in consultation with the State, may designate a public road within the borders of that area of the State as a critical urban freight corridor.

children.heading: .—Urbanized area with a population less than 500,000
children.label: 2 .—Urbanized area with a population less than 500,000
children.text: In an urbanized area with a population of less than 500,000 individuals, the State, in consultation with the representative metropolitan planning organization, may designate a public road within the borders of that area of the State as a critical urban freight corridor.

children.label: A
children.text: is in an urbanized area, regardless of population; and

children.label: I
children.text: the primary highway freight system;

children.label: II
children.text: the Interstate System; or

children.label: III
children.text: an intermodal freight facility;

children.label: ii
children.text: is located within a corridor of a route on the primary highway freight system and provides an alternative highway option important to goods movement;

children.label: iii
children.text: serves a major freight generator, logistic center, or manufacturing and warehouse industrial land; or

children.label: iv
children.text: is important to the movement of freight within the region, as determined by the metropolitan planning organization or the State.

children.heading: .—Limitation
children.label: 4 .—Limitation
children.text: For each State, a maximum of 150 miles of highway or 10 percent of the primary highway freight system mileage in the State, whichever is greater, may be designated as a critical urban freight corridor under paragraphs (1) and (2).

children.heading: .—Designation
children.label: 1 .—Designation
children.text: States and metropolitan planning organizations may designate corridors under subsections (e) and (f) and submit the designated corridors to the Administrator on a rolling basis.

children.heading: .—Certification
children.label: 2 .—Certification
children.text: Each State or metropolitan planning organization that designates a corridor under subsection (e) or (f) shall certify to the Administrator that the designated corridor meets the requirements of the applicable subsection.

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: A State shall obligate funds apportioned to the State under section 104(b)(5) to improve the movement of freight on the National Highway Freight Network.

children.label: A
children.text: the total mileage in the State designated as part of the primary highway freight system; bears to

children.label: B
children.text: the total mileage of the primary highway freight system in all States.

children.heading: .—States with high primary highway freight system mileage
children.label: A .—States with high primary highway freight system mileage
i
children.text: the primary highway freight system;

children.label: ii
children.text: critical rural freight corridors; and

children.label: iii
children.text: critical urban freight corridors.

children.heading: .—States with low primary highway freight system mileage
children.label: B .—States with low primary highway freight system mileage
children.text: If the proportion of a State under paragraph (2) is less than 2 percent, the State may obligate funds apportioned to the State under section 104(b)(5) for projects on any component of the National Highway Freight Network.

children.heading: .—Freight planning
children.label: 4 .—Freight planning
children.text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, effective beginning 2 years after the date of enactment of the FAST Act, a State may not obligate funds apportioned to the State under section 104(b)(5) unless the State has developed a freight plan in accordance with , except that the multimodal component of the plan may be incomplete before an obligation may be made under this section.section 70202 of title 49

children.label: i
children.text: contribute to the efficient movement of freight on the National Highway Freight Network; and

children.label: ii
children.text: be identified in a freight investment plan included in a freight plan of the State that is in effect.

children.label: i
children.text: within the boundaries of public or private freight rail or water facilities (including ports);

children.label: ii
children.text: that provide surface transportation infrastructure necessary to facilitate direct intermodal interchange, transfer, and access into or out of the facility;

children.label: I
children.text: is functionally connected to the National Highway Freight Network; and

children.label: II
children.text: is likely to reduce on-road mobile source emissions; and

children.label: I
children.text: is functionally connected to the National Highway Freight Network; and

children.label: II
children.text: is likely to reduce on-road mobile source emissions.

children.label: i
children.text: Development phase activities, including planning, feasibility analysis, revenue forecasting, environmental review, preliminary engineering and design work, and other preconstruction activities.

children.label: ii
children.text: Construction, reconstruction, rehabilitation, acquisition of real property (including land relating to the project and improvements to land), construction contingencies, acquisition of equipment, and operational improvements directly relating to improving system performance.

children.label: iii
children.text: Intelligent transportation systems and other technology to improve the flow of freight, including intelligent freight transportation systems.

children.label: iv
children.text: Efforts to reduce the environmental impacts of freight movement.

children.label: v
children.text: Environmental and community mitigation for freight movement.

children.label: vi
children.text: Railway-highway grade separation.

children.label: vii
children.text: Geometric improvements to interchanges and ramps.

children.label: viii
children.text: Truck-only lanes.

children.label: ix
children.text: Climbing and runaway truck lanes.

children.label: x
children.text: Adding or widening of shoulders.

children.label: xi
children.text: Truck parking facilities eligible for funding under section 1401 of MAP–21 ( note).23 U.S.C. 137

children.label: xii
children.text: Real-time traffic, truck parking, roadway condition, and multimodal transportation information systems.

children.label: xiii
children.text: Electronic screening and credentialing systems for vehicles, including weigh-in-motion truck inspection technologies.

children.label: xiv
children.text: Traffic signal optimization, including synchronized and adaptive signals.

children.label: xv
children.text: Work zone management and information systems.

children.label: xvi
children.text: Highway ramp metering.

children.label: xvii
children.text: Electronic cargo and border security technologies that improve truck freight movement.

children.label: xviii
children.text: Intelligent transportation systems that would increase truck freight efficiencies inside the boundaries of intermodal facilities.

children.label: xix
children.text: Additional road capacity to address highway freight bottlenecks.

children.label: xx
children.text: Physical separation of passenger vehicles from commercial motor freight.

children.label: xxi
children.text: Enhancement of the resiliency of critical highway infrastructure, including highway infrastructure that supports national energy security, to improve the flow of freight.

children.label: xxii
children.text: A highway or bridge project, other than a project described in clauses (i) through (xxi), to improve the flow of freight on the National Highway Freight Network.

children.label: xxiii
children.text: Any other surface transportation project to improve the flow of freight into and out of a facility described in subparagraph (B).

children.label: A
children.text: carrying out diesel retrofit or alternative fuel projects under section 149 for class 8 vehicles; and

children.label: i
children.text: conducting analyses and data collection related to the national highway freight program;

children.label: ii
children.text: developing and updating performance targets to carry out this section; and

children.label: iii
children.text: reporting to the Administrator to comply with the freight performance target under section 150.

children.heading: .—Applicability of planning requirements
children.label: 7 .—Applicability of planning requirements
children.text: Programming and expenditure of funds for projects under this section shall be consistent with the requirements of sections 134 and 135.
an identification of significant freight system trends, needs, and issues within the State;
a description of the freight policies and strategies that will guide the freight-related transportation investments of the State;
an inventory of freight bottlenecks within the State and a description of the ways in which the State is allocating national highway freight program funds to improve those bottlenecks; and
a description of the actions the State will undertake to meet the performance targets of the State.

children.label: i
children.text: in proximity to, or within, an existing right of way on a Federal-aid highway; or

children.label: ii
children.text: that connect land ports-of entry to existing Federal-aid highways; or1So in original.1

children.label: B
children.text: communications or information processing systems that improve the efficiency, security, or safety of freight movements on the Federal-aid highway system, including to improve the conveyance of freight on dedicated intelligent freight lanes.

children.heading: .—Operating standards
children.label: 2 .—Operating standards
children.text: The Administrator shall determine whether there is a need for establishing operating standards for intelligent freight transportation systems.

children.heading: .—Treatment of Freight Projects
children.label: k .—Treatment of Freight Projects
children.text: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, a freight project carried out under this section shall be treated as if the project were on a Federal-aid highway.
